perturbation.original_text: SFDC hereby appoints SUB-RESELLER ("Sub-Reseller") as a sub-reseller to whom Reseller may resell Services in accordance with Section 2(ii) of the Agreement, provided that Sub-Reseller may only resell such Services to Customer.
perturbation.changed_text: SFDC hereby appoints SUB-RESELLER ("Sub-Reseller") as a sub-reseller to whom Reseller may resell Services in accordance with Section 2(ii) of the Agreement, provided that Sub-Reseller may resell such Services to any entity.
perturbation.explanation: The original text restricts Sub-Resellers to selling only to Customers. The modified text removes this restriction, allowing sales to any entity. This conflicts with standard interpretations of reseller agreements, where territories and customer bases are often clearly defined to avoid market conflicts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Allowing the sub-reseller to sell to 'any entity' without territorial or customer restrictions could potentially lead to anti-competitive practices, such as price fixing or market allocation, violating the Sherman Antitrust Act. The act prohibits agreements that unreasonably restrain trade, and unrestricted resale rights could facilitate such agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty U.S. Code Notes prev | next Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be deemed guilty of a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding $100,000,000 if a corporation, or, if any other person, $1,000,000, or by imprisonment not exceeding 10 years, or by both said punishments, in the discretion of the court. (July 2, 1890, ch. 647, § 1, 26 Stat. 209 ; Aug. 17, 1937, ch. 690, title VIII, 50 Stat. 693 ; July 7, 1955, ch. 281, 69 Stat. 282 ; Pub. L. 93–528, § 3 , Dec. 21, 1974 , 88 Stat. 1708 ; Pub. L. 94–145, § 2 , Dec. 12, 1975 , 89 Stat. 801 ; Pub. L. 101–588, § 4(a) , Nov. 16, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2880 ; Pub. L. 108–237, title II, § 215(a) , June 22, 2004 , 118 Stat. 668 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2004— Pub. L. 108–237 substituted “$100,000,000” for “$10,000,000”, “$1,000,000” for “$350,000”, and “10” for “three”. 1990— Pub. L. 101–588 substituted “$10,000,000” for “one million dollars” and “$350,000” for “one hundred thousand dollars”. 1975— Pub. L. 94–145 struck out from first sentence two provisos granting anti-trust exemption to State fair trade laws. 1974— Pub. L. 93–528 substituted “a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding one million dollars if a corporation, or, if any other person, one hundred thousand dollars, or by imprisonment not exceeding three years” for “a misdemeanor, and on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding f
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by allowing the sub-reseller to sell to 'any entity,' potentially violates the Sherman Antitrust Act, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 U.S. Code § 1, states that 'Every contract, combination... in restraint of trade or commerce... is declared to be illegal.' The unrestricted resale rights granted in the changed text could facilitate agreements that unreasonably restrain trade, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Reseller must ensure that Sub-Reseller complies with the terms of the Agreement applicable to Reseller as if Sub- Reseller were an original party to the Agreement and any breach by Sub-Reseller of the Agreement will be deemed a breach by Reseller.
perturbation.changed_text: Reseller must ensure that Sub-Reseller attempts to comply with the terms of the Agreement applicable to Reseller, and any breach by Sub-Reseller of the Agreement may be deemed a breach by Reseller at SFDC's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "complies" to "attempts to comply" and making the breach determination discretionary, the obligation on the Reseller is significantly weakened. This creates uncertainty about accountability and liability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Agency and Vicarious Liability
perturbation.law_explanation: Under agency law, a principal (Salesforce) can hold an agent (Reseller) liable for the actions of a sub-agent (Sub-Reseller) when the sub-agent acts within the scope of their authority. By making compliance discretionary and breach determination subject to SFDC's whim, the modified text undermines the established legal principle of vicarious liability and the Reseller's responsibility for the Sub-Reseller's actions, potentially shielding SFDC from liability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: agency | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you agency Agency law is a common law doctrine controlling relationships between agents and principals . A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party . The two forms of actual authority include: Express authority : An agent has express authority to take any actions requested by the principal as well as authority to take any actions inherently necessary to accomplish those requests. Implied authority : An agent has implied authority to take any action the principal’s conduct indicates the agent should do. Furthermore, failure by the principal to object to an agent’s prior actions may create implied authority to repeat those actions in the future. An agent does not have implied authority to do anything expressly prohibited by the principal. In addition to actual authority, a principal may be bound by the actions of an agent if apparent authority existed. Apparent authority : An agent has apparent authority when, despite no existing authority, a third party reasonably infers that someone is authorized to act on the principal’s behalf due to the conduct of the principal. Unlike actual authority, a principal can be bound by an agent’s act made with apparent authority even if they explicitly stated that the agent could not do that act. Furthermore, a person possessing a widely recognized title like Hiring Director has apparent authority to accomplish anything a reasonable person would believe that title entails. An agent acting with apparent authority is known as an ostensible agent . Additionally, principals can be held liable for the torts of their agents under the doctrine of vicarious liability . A principal is always liable for torts committed while the agent completes their official responsibilities. For torts occurring outside of official duties , the liability of the principal depends on whether the agent’s tort occurred during a frolic or a detour. A principal is liable for the “detours” of their agent but not for the “frolics.” The primary considerations many courts use to determine if an act was a frolic or a detour include how much control the principal exerts over the agent’s actions and who economically benefits from the agent’s actions. See: Pyne v. Witmer, 129 Ill. 2d 351 (1989) . perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the Reseller's obligation to ensure the Sub-Reseller's compliance, potentially shielding SFDC from liability. By changing 'complies' to 'attempts to comply' and making breach determination discretionary, the original contract's vicarious liability principle is undermined, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'principals can be held liable for the torts of their agents under the doctrine of vicarious liability'. The original contract ensures that the Reseller is responsible for the Sub-Reseller's compliance, fitting under this liability. However, the changed text introduces uncertainty, possibly allowing SFDC to avoid responsibility by arguing that the Reseller only needed to 'attempt' compliance, contradicting agency law principles.

perturbation.original_text: The terms and conditions herein contained constitute the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter of this Sub-Reseller Agreement and supersede any previous and contemporaneous agreements and understandings, whether oral or written, between the Parties hereto with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The terms and conditions herein contained constitute the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to some aspects of the subject matter of this Sub-Reseller Agreement, but do not supersede any previous and contemporaneous agreements and understandings, whether oral or written, between the Parties hereto with respect to other aspects of the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause is a standard 'entire agreement' or 'integration' clause. The modified clause undermines this, suggesting that other agreements may still be in effect, which creates ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Parol Evidence Rule generally prevents parties from introducing evidence of prior or contemporaneous agreements to contradict or vary the terms of a fully integrated written contract. By stating that the agreement only covers 'some aspects' and doesn't supersede other agreements, the modified text directly contradicts the purpose of an integration clause and weakens the enforceability of the written agreement by allowing external evidence to be considered in disputes, undermining the rule's intended effect of providing certainty in contractual obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented (a) by course of dealing or usage of trade (Section 1-205 ) or by course of performance (Section 2-208 ); and (b) by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the purpose of an integration clause, potentially violating the Parol Evidence Rule. The original clause ensures the contract is the 'entire agreement,' preventing external agreements from influencing interpretation. The changed text, however, states the agreement covers only 'some aspects' and doesn't supersede other agreements, directly contradicting the original intent and undermining the enforceability of the written agreement, as it allows external evidence. Snippet 1 supports this: 'Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement.' The changed text opens the door for such contradictions, creating a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.
